context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Development Theories Today in the Context of Global Crisis

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Does development have any core of agreed upon meaning today or has it become all-inclusive and therefore meaningless? How can one think of success in development terms today and which countries or communities would be role models? Is today's global crisis one created by too much markets or government or too little and if so what scale?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m trying to figure out this reflection paper on development theories and the global crisis. I’m stuck on the main argument, especially whether “development” still has a clear meaning or if it’s become too broad.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — that tension is really at the heart of the prompt. So let me ask you: when you hear the word 'development,' what comes to mind? What do you think it means?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh and has identified the core tension of the prompt. Rather than explaining the debate to them, I should use a Socratic question to get them to activate their own thinking about what 'development' means to them first. This will help them build their argument from their own reasoning rather than from my scaffolding.

exchanges.student: My first instinct is economic growth, like GDP going up, but I’m pretty sure that’s too narrow for this class. I think it also includes poverty reduction, institutions, maybe participation and legitimacy, but then it starts to feel so broad that I’m not sure what the “core” is anymore.
exchanges.tutor: You've landed right on the dilemma the prompt is asking you to resolve. So here's the key question: do YOU think that broadening beyond GDP makes the concept meaningless, or do you think there's still a defensible core? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently identified the key tension between narrow (GDP) and broad definitions of development, which is excellent. They're essentially restating the prompt's dilemma in their own words. Rather than validating and moving on, I should push them to take a position - this is a reflection paper and they need a clear argument. A Socratic question pushing them toward committing to a stance is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: I think there’s still a core, but I can’t tell if I’m just saying that because “meaningless” sounds too extreme. Maybe the core is improving people’s actual life chances, and the broad stuff like institutions or participation matters because it affects that?
exchanges.tutor: That's an interesting candidate for a core. Now stress-test it: can you think of a situation where life chances improved but we'd hesitate to call it 'development'? Or the reverse?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress - they've proposed a potential core concept (improving life chances) and even started to articulate why broader factors matter instrumentally. This is good substantive thinking. I should push them to test and sharpen this idea rather than just accepting it, using a Socratic question that challenges the robustness of their proposed core.

exchanges.student: Maybe if incomes rise a lot but it comes from super exploitative labor or extreme inequality, I’d hesitate to call that development, or at least successful development. And the reverse could be a place with stronger local participation or rights, but not much economic growth yet, where I’d still want to call that development in some sense.
exchanges.tutor: You've done some solid thinking there. Now, given that framework you're building, which countries or communities would you point to as role models for 'success' in development today?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well and has essentially built their own argument for a multidimensional core. They've identified that neither pure economic growth nor pure participation alone is sufficient. This is good progress. I should now connect this to the second part of the prompt - success and role models - to keep moving the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking maybe South Korea for rapid transformation, but that feels kind of classic/developmental-state and maybe not “today.” Maybe Kerala too, since it gets used a lot for social development without being super rich, but I’m not fully sure how to connect that to the global crisis part.
exchanges.tutor: Those are reasonable examples to work with. But you flagged something important — the global crisis. In your view, what caused it: too much market, too much government, too little of either, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good instincts on examples and is self-aware about their limitations. They're also flagging the connection to the global crisis part of the prompt, which is the third component they need to address. I should acknowledge their examples briefly but pivot to the global crisis question since that's where they feel stuck and it's a key part of the prompt they haven't tackled yet.

exchanges.student: My gut says too much market at the global financial level, like deregulation or letting finance run too far. But I also think “too little government” might be the sharper way to say it, because it’s not just markets existing, it’s weak regulation at the national and maybe global scale.
exchanges.tutor: You're touching on something the prompt specifically asks about — scale. When you say weak regulation, at what scale do you think the failure happened most critically, and does that matter for how development planning should respond?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a nuanced and interesting point - framing it as 'too little government' (regulation) rather than 'too much market' is actually a meaningful analytical distinction worth pushing on. They're also touching on scale, which is explicitly in the prompt. I should push them to think about the scale dimension since they've already hinted at it.

exchanges.student: I think mostly national and global scales? National because states regulate banks and markets, but global because capital moves across borders way faster than regulation does. I’m a little fuzzy on how to turn that into a clean thesis though.
exchanges.tutor: You actually have all the ingredients now. Try drafting your thesis in one or two sentences — don't worry about polish, just capture the argument you've built.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked through all three parts of the prompt through their own reasoning. They have the building blocks for a thesis. Rather than helping them formulate it (which would cross into writing for them), I should ask them to attempt articulating their thesis themselves based on what they've developed.

exchanges.student: Maybe: development still has a meaningful core if it’s understood as expanding people’s real life chances in ways that are socially just and politically legitimate, not just increasing GDP. The global crisis showed that markets without strong national and global governance produce unstable and unequal outcomes, so successful development today should be judged by both human well-being and the institutions that make it sustainable.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong thesis — it addresses all three parts of the prompt and reflects the reasoning you built. One thing to consider: your paper will need to defend 'politically legitimate' as part of the core — where does that come from in your argument? Can you connect it back to something concrete?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid thesis that addresses all three parts of the prompt. It's their own work and shows real thinking. Rather than praising extensively or suggesting language improvements (which would violate the rules), I should give honest feedback on its merits and push them to think about any gaps before they start writing.

exchanges.student: I think from the course theme that planning is tied to power and legitimacy, so development isn’t just outcomes imposed from above. Maybe concretely, if growth happens through authoritarian or exclusionary policies, it can improve some indicators but still be fragile or unequal, so legitimacy matters because it affects who benefits and whether change lasts.
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-grounded connection. You've now built a coherent argument across all three parts of the prompt. I'd say it's time to start writing — you have a thesis, examples, and reasoning. What's your plan for structuring the 700-800 words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong connection to course content and extended their argument well. They've now worked through all parts of the prompt with solid reasoning. We're at roughly 8 exchanges and the student has everything they need to write. I should acknowledge this progress and redirect them toward actually writing the paper, since continuing to discuss risks redundancy.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking intro with the thesis, then one paragraph on why development still has a core beyond GDP, one on success/role models like Kerala and maybe South Korea, and one on the global crisis as a governance failure across scales. I’m not totally sure if that’s too compressed, though.
exchanges.tutor: That structure maps cleanly onto the prompt. The only way to know if it's too compressed is to start writing and see where you run out of space. Go ahead and draft it — you're ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear and reasonable structure that maps onto the prompt's three questions. The concern about compression is valid but ultimately something they need to figure out through writing. I shouldn't over-scaffold at this point - they should just start writing and see. A brief nudge to start is appropriate here, our planned 10 exchanges are nearly complete.
